claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who is 16 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 16 years old, I need to ask you an important question about their current situation.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs, or similar qualifications)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They left school and aren't doing any courses right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I have a follow-up question about Alex's current situation.

Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services? (You can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either of these.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex hasn't registered with the careers service or anything like that. They just left school and haven't signed up for any of those services.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have one more question to complete my assessment.

Is Alex currently working, and if so, how many hours per week do they work?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for answering all my questions. Let me assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit based on the information you've provided.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid you would **not be eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.

Here's why: Child Benefit normally continues for children aged 16 or over only if they are in approved education or training (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or similar qualifications). Alternatively, you can claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services.

Since Alex is not currently in approved education or training and has not registered with a careers service or the armed services, the eligibility criteria are not met.

**However**, if Alex's circumstances change - for example, if they enroll in education or training, or register with a careers service - you may become eligible at that point. If that happens, you would be able to make a new claim for Child Benefit.

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and is not currently in approved education or training. According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16, OR if the child is under 20 and stays in approved education or training. Additionally, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. However, Alex has not registered with either of these services. Since Alex is 16, not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or the armed services, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 16 years old. Alex is not in approved education or training, has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or armed services, and is not working. Under the Child Benefit rules, eligibility for a 16-year-old requires them to be in approved education or training, or to have registered with specific services to qualify for a 20-week extension period. Since neither condition is met, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16, OR if the child is under 20 and stays in approved education or training. Additionally, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. However, Alex has not registered with either of these services. Since Alex is 16, not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or the armed services, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.

Alex is not in approved education or training, has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or armed services, and is not working. Under the Child Benefit rules, eligibility for a 16-year-old requires them to be in approved education or training, or to have registered with specific services to qualify for a 20-week extension period. Since neither condition is met, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
